channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Supreme Court Lifts Spending Limits on Political Parties and Candidates
item.description: Republicans had asked the court to strike down restrictions on how much political parties can spend in coordination with candidates.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Adam Liptak
item.category: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission (Supreme Court Decision)
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Delegates and supporters at the Republican National Convention in 2024. The National Republican Senatorial Committee brought a legal challenge against limits on coordinated spending when JD Vance ran for Senate in 2022.

item.title: The Supreme Court Just Gave the G.O.P. a New Midterm Edge
item.description: The decision, which allows parties to spend more in coordination with candidates, is likely to further expand the power of big money in American politics.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.category: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission (Supreme Court Decision)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s ruling on party spending restrictions was expected to give Republicans a significant financial advantage in this year’s midterms.

item.title: What to Know About the Supreme Court’s Birthright Citizenship Order
item.description: The decision ends one of the most aggressive parts of Trump’s immigration agenda. But hundreds of other restrictions have taken effect.
item.dc:creator: Jazmine Ulloa
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court ruled against the president 6 to 3.

item.title: Stocks Notch Strongest Quarter Since 2020
item.description: The S&P 500 rose almost 15 percent for the three months through June, and many stock analysts remain optimistic that corporate earnings driven by artificial intelligence will keep growing.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Tom Kean, New Jersey Congressman, Says Depression Led to Long Absence
item.description: After months of mystery, the New Jersey representative broke his silence about the undisclosed health issue that prompted his 117-day absence.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas H. Kean Jr., a New Jersey Republican, at the Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: House G.O.P. Revolt Blocks Pentagon Bill
item.description: Ultraconservative lawmakers refused to back a critical procedural measure as they pressed for action on voting legislation championed by President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison, Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Speaker Mike Johnson, center, has struggled to corral his majority to act on critical legislation on the Pentagon, spending and other matters.

item.title: Texas Is a Tossup. The Times/Siena Poll Points to How It Got There.
item.description: A major shift among Hispanic voters and a favorable candidate matchup have helped put Democrats on the doorstep of a Senate upset.
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Talarico received positive ratings on every question asked in the survey.

item.title: 4 Warning Signs for Graham Platner. And 4 for Susan Collins Too.
item.description: The Maine race, which both parties see as key to winning control of the Senate, is extremely close, a Times/Portland Press Herald/Siena poll found. Both candidates have reasons for concern.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times; Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: What to Watch in Colorado Primary Elections: Will Left-Wing Energy Keep Rising?
item.description: Primaries on Tuesday will be the latest test of Democrats’ anti-establishment mood.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Chet Strange for The New York Times; David Zalubowski/Associated Press

item.title: States Sue Trump Administration Over Medicaid Work Requirements
item.description: The lawsuit argues that new federal rules went beyond what Congress enacted and broke from guidance that the federal government previewed to states.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff and Margot Sanger-Katz
item.category: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration is being sued on the new Medicaid work requirements, arguing that the guidelines are inconsistent with federal law.

item.title: C.I.A. Reorganization Prioritizes Cyberoperations
item.description: John Ratcliffe, the C.I.A. director, said that the agency would take “smart risks,” but that people would have oversight of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: John Ratcliffe, the C.I.A. director, said the overhaul was an acknowledgment that in the modern world, digital borders are as important as physical borders.

item.title: Court Halts Pentagon Rule Requiring Escorts for Journalists
item.description: A federal judge temporarily blocked a new rule that restricted reporters’ access to the Pentagon, in a lawsuit brought by The New York Times.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Times challenged the Pentagon’s rule as an unconstitutional infringement of the First Amendment.

item.title: After U.S.-Iran War, Oman Is Said to Propose Strait of Hormuz Fee Plan
item.description: Under the proposal, Iran and Oman would collect payment for ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz, according to officials and diplomats with knowledge of the matter.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim, Farnaz Fassihi and Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images
item.media:description: A cargo vessel anchored off the coast of Oman near Muscat last week.

item.title: The Founding Father Who Sought a Last-Minute Deal to Avert the Revolution
item.description: Recently discovered documents reveal a back-channel attempt by a leader of the Continental Congress to make peace. (He did not succeed.)
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall
item.media:credit: Lauren O'Neil for The New York Times
item.media:description: Letters written by John Dickinson, a member of the Continental Congress, displayed in Cambridge, Mass.

item.title: Record-Breaking Heat Wave Expected to Hit New York City Ahead of July 4 Weekend
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani signed an executive order that aims to protect workers who must toil outside. Temperatures this week could exceed 100 degrees.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Goldstein
item.media:credit: Adam Gray/Associated Press
item.media:description: New York City endured a bout of extreme heat in May, but a more intense stretch is forecast for this week.

item.title: Fourth of July Heat Wave Forecast: Here Are the Day-by-Day Temperatures
item.description: A day-by-day breakdown of where dangerous heat is expected through the Fourth of July weekend.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar, Judson Jones and Erin McCann
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Ontario and Quebec Face Heat Warnings as High Temperatures Spread Across Canada
item.description: Parts of Ontario and Quebec will be well above normal temperatures this week as a heat wave spreads over North America.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:credit: Michael Steele/Getty Images
item.media:description: Toronto and other parts of Canada will face higher temperatures.

item.title: Bending Spoons, Owner of AOL and Other Old Internet Brands, Is Going Public
item.description: Bending Spoons, an Italian company that buys aging internet companies, is going public this week at a potential value of $19 billion.
item.dc:creator: Erin Griffith
item.media:credit: Josie Norton

item.title: How Grindr’s C.E.O. Adopted A.I.: ‘I Just Imposed It’
item.description: George Arison, the gay dating app’s chief executive, is aiming for all code to be eventually written by artificial intelligence, making the company “leaner.”
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: George Arison founded two companies before arriving at Grindr in 2022.

item.title: Mamdani and City Council Agree on $126 Billion Budget
item.description: A handshake deal, reached right at the statutory budget deadline, will expand programs that offer housing vouchers and discounted transit fares.
item.dc:creator: Sally Goldenberg and Dana Rubinstein
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani and the City Council speaker, Julie Menin, held days of late-stage negotiations that were often rancorous before reaching agreement.

item.title: The Prom Went On in Kyiv, but Masha’s Date Danced Alone
item.description: Masha Polska, 15, was an avid dancer who had been dreaming of a star turn in the group waltz. That was not to be.
item.dc:creator: Siobhán O’Grady, Liubov Sholudko and Oksana Parafeniuk
item.media:description: Yehor Holodryha, center, and classmates of Masha Polska dancing the waltz at their prom in Kyiv, Ukraine, in June.

item.title: On the ‘Lux Tour’, Rosalía Shape Shifts Through Dance
item.description: On the “Lux” tour, the pop star mingles dance genres, starting the show on pointe. As with her dip into opera, her technique might not be perfect, but the intention is good.
item.dc:creator: Margaret Fuhrer
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: The Supreme Court Gets Birthright Citizenship Right
item.description: The Constitution is clear. People who are born in this country and subject to its laws are citizens.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board
item.media:credit: Damon Winter/The New York Times

item.title: I’m Gay, Not Queer. It Matters.
item.description: Polyamorous? Queer. Vaguely uncomfortable with gender expectations? Possibly queer, too.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Vines
item.category: Same-Sex Marriage, Civil Unions and Domestic Partnerships
item.media:credit: Matthew Leifheit

item.title: With New Seizures, Value of the Met’s Looted Artifacts Tops $95 Million
item.description: The Manhattan district attorney’s office said it secured the return of dozens of ancient artifacts in June, after a process that the museum described as collaborative.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley and Tom Mashberg
